route: Endotracheally followed by manual bag ventilation
preparation: Administer ET undiluted
administration: Use the IV/IO route if available rather than the endotracheal route. As soon as an IV/IO route is obtained, administer a dose of IV epinephrine even if the last dose of ET epinephrine dates back less than 3 minutes. Repeat every q3-5min in cardiac arrest
particularity: Avoid 1:10,000 nomenclature as it is associated with more medication errors.

administration: Preferred concentration in children under 2 Kg = 10 mcg/mL, between 2 and 4 Kg = 20 mcg/mL or over 4 Kg or in fluid restriction = 40 mcg/mL. Use the lowest concentration if a peripheral venous access is used.  In cardiogenic shock, the reported doses are 0.02-0.1 mcg/Kg/min. In distributive septic shock, the reported doses are 0.05-1 mcg/Kg/min. In practice, the addition of the vasoconstrictor effect (alpha) to the inotropic effect (beta) is not precise and absolute.  Suggested titration: 1) Start infusion at 0.05 mcg/kg/min. 2) Titrate by increments of 0.01-0.02 mcg/kg/min q5min depending on clinical response. (Expert Opinion)  Do NOT remove volume during preparation. Rinse the tubing with the infusion before administration. Epinephrine can also be diluted in NaCl 0.9% or D10W.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.  Avoid 1:1000 nomenclature as it is associated with more medication errors.  Although the preferred route of administration of vasopressors is via central venous access, multiple studies demonstrate that administration via a good quality peripheral access is safe if used for a short period of time and with frequent verifications of the access site.

preparation: Administer IV diluted up to 1 mL of NaCl 0.9%
administration: Increased risk of chest wall rigidity in neonates linked to the rate of administration and high doses. In the context of intubation, the use of a neuromuscular blocker treats chest wall rigidity.  Consider dose adjustment according to hemodynamic status  Dose: 3-5 mcg/kg (usually 4 mcg/Kg) if used as the ONLY induction agent in rapid sequence intubation
particularity: Nil

preparation: Administer IV undiluted
administration: In neonatology, succinylcholine is the neuromuscular blocker usually used for intubations. Contraindications to succinylcholine are only often revealed after birth and intubation. Rocuronium can also be used as an alternative neuromuscular blocking agent.
particularity: Keep refrigerated. Stable at room temperature for 6 months.  With dose of 1.5-2 mg/Kg: Onset of action: 15 seconds Duration of action: 4-6 minutes

administration: Use a good caliber proximal venous access such as the right upper extremity elbow crease. Administration via a distal peripheral route (eg: access on the foot) decreases the chances of success and may require higher doses (eg: 0.3 mg/Kg).  Ideally administer while recording a 12-lead ECG. Defibrillator and resuscitation cart nearby.  Double-syringe or 3-way stopcock administration technique:  Preparation: 1) Withdraw the dose of adenosine, with an additional 0.3 mL, with a 1 or 3 mL syringe 2) Open the valve to inlet 1 (inlet 2 blocked, patient outlet open) 3) Screw the adenosine syringe onto inlet 1 and purge the tap with 0.3 mL of adenosine 4) Adjust the volume of adenosine to the desired dose in the 1 or 3 mL syringe 5) Block entry 1 (adenosine) 6) Screw a 5 mL syringe of NaCl 0.9% onto inlet 2 7) Purge the 3-way stopcock with approximately 2 mL of NaCl 0.9%  Administration: 1) Connect the output of the device to the patient 2) Open the valve to inlet 1 (adenosine) and administer the adenosine in 1-3 seconds 3) Open the valve to inlet 2 (NaCl 0.9%) and rinse QUICKLY with 1-3 mL of NaCl 0.9%
particularity: Consider reducing the dose by 50% if:  - Administrated via central venous access

preparation: Administer IV by double-syringe technique
administration: Use a good caliber proximal venous access such as the right upper extremity elbow crease. Administration via a distal peripheral route (eg: access on the foot) decreases the chances of success and may require higher doses (eg: 0.3 mg/Kg).  Ideally administer while recording a 12-lead ECG. Defibrillator and resuscitation cart nearby.  Double-syringe or 3-way stopcock administration technique:  BEFORE connecting it to the patient (Priming): 1) Withdraw the dose of adenosine, with an additional 0.3 mL, with a 1 or 3 mL syringe 2) Open the valve to inlet 1 (inlet 2 blocked, patient outlet open) 3) Screw the adenosine syringe onto inlet 1 and purge the tap with 0.3 mL of adenosine 4) Adjust the volume of adenosine to the desired dose in the 1 or 3 mL syringe 5) Block entry 1 (adenosine) 6) Screw a 5 mL syringe of NaCl 0.9% onto inlet 2 7) Purge the 3-way stopcock with approximately 2 mL of NaCl 0.9%  Administration of the dose: 1) Connect the output of the device to the patient 2) Open the valve to inlet 1 (adenosine) and administer the adenosine in 1-3 seconds 3) Open the valve to inlet 2 (NaCl 0.9%) and rinse QUICKLY with 3 mL of NaCl 0.9%
particularity: Consider reducing the dose by 50% if:  - Administrated via central venous access

preparation: Administer IV diluted up to 1 mL of NaCl 0.9%
administration: Caution: • Consider dose adjustment according to hemodynamic status • Ketamine may be favored in patients kept with spontaneous breathing (ex.: administration of surfactant by less invasive method) or with hemodynamic instability.  Do NOT use ketamine 50 mg/mL undiluted; unmeasurable volumes for neonatal doses If 10 mg/mL concentration is unavailable: Presentation : Vial of Ketamine 50 mg/mL 1) Withdraw 2 mL (1 vial) of Ketamine 50 mg/mL with a 10 mL syringe 2) Add to 8 mL of NaCl 0.9% to the syringe 3) Final concentration = 100 mg in 10 mL = 10 mg/mL
particularity: Laryngospasm risk < 1%

administration: Caution: Use the lowest concentration if a peripheral venous access is used.  Preferred concentration: • Under 1 Kg = 800 mcg/mL • 1 and 3 Kg = 1600 mcg/mL • Over 3 Kg or in fluid restriction = 3200 mcg/mL.   Suggested titration: 1) Start infusion at 5 mcg/kg/min. 2) Titrate by increments of 2.5-5 mcg/kg/min q5min until target BP is reached or a maximum dose of 20 mcg/Kg/min. (Expert Opinion)  Additional information: Rinse the tubing with the infusion before administration.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.  Starting dose 5 mcg/Kg/min ("renal" dose of 2-3 mcg/Kg/min not effective according to the literature).  Although the preferred route of administration of vasopressors is via central venous access, multiple studies demonstrate that administration via a good quality peripheral access is safe if used for a short period of time and with frequent verifications of the access site.

administration: Caution: • Opinion of Pediatric Cardiology or Neonatology recommended • Preferred concentration :• Under 3 Kg = 5 mcg/mL  • Over 3 Kg = 10 mcg/mL  Suggested titration: 1) Start the infusion at 0.03-0.05 mcg/Kg/min. 2) Titrate by increments of 0.03-0.05 mcg/Kg/min according to the O2 saturation and/or arterial pressure objectives discussed with the experts, according to the suspected cardiopathy, to open or maintain the ductus arteriosus. 3) Once the ductus arteriosus is open and the patient is hemodynamically stabilized, titrate to obtain the minimum effective dose and minimize side effects. (e.g.: Decrease to 0.05 mcg/Kg/min then 0.02 mcg/Kg/min q6h ad 0.01 mcg/Kg/min according to echocardiography) (Expert opinion)  Additional information: • Do NOT remove volume during preparation. • Rinse the tubing with the infusion before administration. • Alprostadil can also be diluted in NaCl 0.9% or D10W. • If patient with reduced dextrose intake, very at risk of hypoglycemia or with a history of hypoglycemia, dilute in D10W rather than D5W.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.  Keep refrigerated.  Monitor for hypotension, apnea and fever.

preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 1 mL of NaCl 0.9% after reconstitution
administration: Reconstitute according to the manufacturer's recommendations. IV administration over 3-5 minutes is safe according to the literature. It is possible to administer the drug over a longer period according to the hospital protocols in place. Secondary dilution not necessary. Can be administered IM.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.  The first dose of an anti-infective medication (mg) should not be influenced by the presence or absence of renal insufficiency in order to achieve an effective concentration quickly. If adjustments are necessary, these should be applied at subsequent doses.  Dosage adjustment in renal failure.  Usual neonatal dosing according to postmenstrual and postnatal age: (Postmenstrual age = gestational age + postnatal age)  Postmenstrual age < 30 weeks: 0 to 28 days : q12h More than 28 days : q8h  Postmenstrual age 30 à 36 weeks: 0 to 14 days : q12h More than 14 days : q8h  Postmenstrual age ≥ 37 weeks: 0 to 7 days : q12h 8 to 14 days : q8h More than 14 days : q6h (maximum 300 mg/kg/day)

preparation: Administer IV undiluted
administration: IV push in cardiac arrest  If the patient is hemodynamically stable, administer diluted over 30 minutes to reduce the risk of cerebral hemorrhage.  Administer preferably via proximal peripheral venous access if central access not available (hyperosmolar solution).  Hyperkaliemia in neonatology is defined as K+> 6.5-7.5 mmol/L. Neonates often tolerate blood levels of K+ higher than children and adults. In doubt, control the K+ level and look for anomalies on the 12-lead EKG.  If Sodium Bicarbonate 4.2% (0.5mmol/mL) is unavailable: Presentation : Vial or syringe of Sodium Bicarbonate 8.4% (1 mmol/mL) 1) Withdraw 5 mL of Bicarbonate 1 mmol/mL with a 10 mL syringe 2) Add to 5 mL of Sterile water for injection or D5W to the syringe 3) Final concentration = 5 mmol in 10 mL = 0.5 mmol/mL = 4.2%
particularity: Incompatible in Y with Calcium or Epinephrine. Rinse well with NaCl 0.9% between administrations

preparation: Administer undiluted on volume/time pump
administration: Thresholds for treatment of hypoglycaemia: Birth to 72 hours of age : 2.6 mmol/L ≥72 hours of age: 3.3 mmol/L  If D10% is unavailable: Presentation : Vial of D5% 1) Withdraw 63 mL of D50% 2) Add to 500 mL bag of D5%  3) Do NOT remove volume during preparation. 4) Final concentration : D10% 5) Rinse the tubing with the infusion before administration  6) Enter the required volume on the pump and administer over 5-15 minutes.  Administration of intrabuccal glucose gel while trying to establish IV access is a reasonable approach. If any glucose gel is unavailable, D50% intrabuccal can be used, but administer slowly, as there is a risk of choking.

preparation: Add to 2mL of NaCl 0.9%
administration: Rapid IV administration may cause anaphylactoid reactions with possible hypotension. In the context of prophylaxis, administer over 30 minutes.  Despite the Canadian Pediatric Society's lack of position on IV vitamin K, consider IV administration in premature infants less than 28 weeks of gestation. IM administration in the thigh is difficult due to low muscle mass.
particularity: Protect from light

preparation: Administer IV undiluted or diluted up to 1 mL of NaCl 0,9%
administration: Attention: • Different concentrations available • Usual neonatal dosing according to postmenstrual and postnatal age: (Postmenstrual age = gestational age + postnatal age)  Neonatal dosing: Postmenstrual age < 30 weeks: 0 to 28 days : 3.8 mg/Kg q36h More than 28 days : 3.2 mg/Kg q24h  Postmenstrual age 30 à 39 weeks: 0 to 28 days : 3.2 mg/Kg q24h More than 28 days : 3 mg/Kg q18h  Postmenstrual age ≥ 40 weeks: 0 to 7 days : 3 mg/Kg q18h 7 to 28 days : 3 mg/Kg q12h For infants over 28 days, see dosing in Pediatric section  Additional information: • IV administration over 3-5 minutes is safe according to the literature. • It is possible to administer the drug over a longer period according to the hospital protocols in place. • Secondary dilution not necessary. • Can be administered IM.
particularity: Y-site compatible with D5W, NaCl, LR.   Because of the risk of nephrotoxicity, consider another class of antibiotics in patients with CrCl less than 30 mL/min or in acute renal failure.  The first dose of an anti-infective medication (mg) should not be influenced by the presence or absence of renal insufficiency in order to achieve an effective concentration quickly. If adjustments are necessary, these should be applied at subsequent doses.  Dosage adjustment in renal failure.

preparation: Rate of administration varies depending on the clinical indication
administration: Repeat according to clinical response.  Transfusion threshold in hemorrhage or transport : platelets < 50 x 10^9/L. (Adapt to the clinical situation with the recommendations of neonatology.)  In neonatology, prescribe transfusions in mL rather than units. 1 platelets unit (apheresis) = approximately 250 mL
particularity: Nil

preparation: Administer on volume/time pump
administration: For patients < 32 weeks, if the clinical condition allows, favor administration over at least 10 minutes to reduce the risk of intraventricular hemorrhage.  Repeat as needed with clinical reassessment between each bolus. In the absence of confirmed neonatal anemia, consider early administration of vasopressors after 1 bolus.
particularity: Nil

administration: Usual fluid requirements in the first 24 hours of life. After 24 hours, consider adding electrolytes to the D10W in the absence of renal insufficiency. Daily increase of 10 to 20 mL/kg/day. Target fluid requirements at 5–6 days of life: 150–160 mL/kg/day (up to 180 mL/kg/day in very premature infants)

indication: Respiratory distress syndrome of the newborn
preparation: By multi-access endotracheal catheter by classic or less invasive method
administration: Attention: • Opinion of Neonatology recommended • Administration with respiratory therapist • Avoid endotracheal aspiration within 2 hours after administration.  Technique: 1) Warm the vial at room temperature for 20 minutes or by placing it in the hands for 5 minutes,  2) Gently swirl, without shaking, to obtain a uniform whitish suspension. 3) Gently flush the endotracheal catheter with 1 mL of air after administration to avoid risk of obstruction of the endotracheal tube.  4) Adjust ventilatory parameters : Lung compliance and oxygenation may improve very quickly after instillation; it is essential to quickly adjust the ventilatory parameters to avoid hyperoxia and pulmonary distension. Risk of pneumothorax if pressure ventilation.  Additional informations: • 1st dose of 200 mg/Kg, repeat a 2nd and 3rd dose of 100 mg/Kg every 12 hours if necessary. • Natural porcine lung surfactant • Salvage treatment of idiopathic respiratory distress syndrome (RDS) of premature newborns (> 25 weeks), neonatal RDS associated with meconium aspiration, pulmonary hamorrhage or pneumonia
particularity: Keep refrigerated Protect from light Unopened vial can be stored for a maximum of 24 hours at room temperature and can be refrigerated again (once only)
reference_snippet: - Ng EH, Shah V. Les directives pour le traitement par surfactant exogène chez le nouveau-né. Paediatrics & Child Health. 2021 Feb;26(1):42-9. - Aldana-Aguirre JC, Pinto M, Featherstone RM, et al. Less Invasive Surfactant Administration Versus Intubation for Surfactant Delivery in Preterm Infants Wi
